w:r.w:t: Technology Stack - Coding Standards - Folder Structure - Logging - Caching - Queueing
Vol. 1 Business Architecture; Vol. 2 Application Architecture (16 bounded contexts CTX-01...CTX-16, module dependency graph, event catalog)
Vol. 4 Data Architecture; Vol. 5 Integration Architecture; Vol. 6 Security Architecture; Vol. 7 Infrastructure & Deployment; Vol. 8 Testing & QA; Vol. 9 UI/UX & API Design

This volume selects and justifies the concrete technology stack, and defines the engineering conventions (folder structure, coding standards, logging, caching, queueing, multi-tenancy implementation) needed to start writing code against the bounded contexts fixed in Volume 2. Every decision below is written as a lightweight ADR (Architecture Decision Record): context, decision, and consequences, so it can be revisited explicitly rather than silently drifted from.
Vol. 1 Principle P1: multi-tenancy is first-class - every table, cache key, queue message, and log line carries a tenant identifier (Section 6, 7, 8, 11 of this volume implement this).
Vol. 1 Principle P3/P4: the ledger is the single source of truth and is append-only/auditable - this constrains the database and ORM choices in Section 2.
Vol. 2 Section 2 decision: modular monolith first, with enforced module boundaries - this constrains the folder structure (Section 3) and coding standards (Section 4).
Vol. 2 Section 10 event catalog: the platform needs a durable, ordered-per-key event bus - this constrains Section 8's queueing technology choice.

Each decision below follows the same format: Context, Options Considered, Decision, Rationale, Consequences. Where the choice affects cost or ops complexity, that trade-off is stated explicitly rather than assumed.
TypeScript on Node.js, using NestJS as the application framework

NestJS's module system maps directly onto Vol. 2's bounded-context boundaries (each CTX-xx becomes a NestJS module with an explicit exported provider surface, enforcing "no cross-module direct database access"). TypeScript end-to-end (backend + frontend, Section 2.3) lets domain types (Vol. 2 Section 8 UML) be shared as a single source of truth via a shared types package, eliminating an entire class of integration bugs. NestJS has first-class support for dependency injection, decorators-based validation, and modular testing, all of which Section 4/Vol. 8 rely on.

Team must standardize on Node.js performance patterns for CPU-heavy work (e.g. large report generation, Section 9) - offload those to worker processes/queues rather than blocking the main event loop. Node's single-threaded model is not a concern for typical I/O-bound accounting operations.
PostgreSQL; MySQL; SQL Server; MongoDB (document store)

Financial data is inherently relational with strict integrity constraints (Vol. 2 Section 8 double-entry model); PostgreSQL's transactional guarantees, mature JSON column support (for custom fields, Vol. 1 BC-17), Row-Level Security (Section 11 multi-tenancy), and extension ecosystem (e.g. pg_partman for partitioning large ledger tables) make it the strongest fit. A document store was rejected because financial reporting requires strong joins and aggregate consistency that document stores handle poorly at this integrity bar.
Vol. 4 must design partitioning/indexing strategy for high-volume tables (JournalLine, InvoiceLineItem) from day one, since these grow unbounded per tenant.
React with TypeScript, using a component library built on a headless UI kit (e.g. Radix primitives) rather than a heavyweight admin template

Largest talent pool and ecosystem; shares TypeScript types with the NestJS backend (Section 2.1); React Query (or equivalent) gives a clean pattern for the API-first architecture (Vol. 2 Principle: UI is a client of the same API third parties use).

Vol. 9 (UI/UX Design Specification) must define the component library and design tokens before feature teams start building screens, to avoid inconsistent UI drift across the ~16 bounded-context-aligned feature areas.

Industry-standard, supports the cache patterns in Section 7 (read-through caching, rate limiting, session storage, distributed locks for e.g. invoice numbering sequences), and is a dependency most cloud providers offer managed.

Apache Kafka (or managed equivalent, e.g. Confluent Cloud/MSK) for the domain event bus (Vol. 2 Section 10); RabbitMQ-style task queues are used underneath for simple background jobs (Section 9) where Kafka's log-retention model is unnecessary overhead

Financial domain events (JournalPosted, InvoiceSent) benefit from Kafka's durable, replayable log - this gives natural audit trail redundancy (Vol. 1 Principle P4) and lets a new consumer (e.g. a future Reporting read-model rebuild) replay history rather than requiring a backfill migration. Per-key ordering (partitioned by tenant + aggregate ID) preserves causal order of events for a given invoice or journal entry.

Requires operating a Kafka cluster (or paying for a managed one) from Release 1 even while the application itself is a single monolith process - this is accepted because event-driven decoupling (Vol. 2 Section 2.2) is needed from day one, not just after service extraction.
OpenSearch (Elasticsearch-compatible), fed by the Kafka event stream, used for the Reporting & Analytics module's (CTX-11) full-text and aggregate query needs

PostgreSQL read replicas alone are insufficient for the custom-report-builder and cross-module aggregate queries in Vol. 1 BC-13; a denormalized, event-sourced search index keeps Reporting's read-only, downstream position (Vol. 2 Section 3.3) without adding query load to the transactional database.
S3-compatible object storage (AWS S3 or self-hosted MinIO for on-prem/dev), used by the Documents context (CTX-12) for attachments and by Reporting for exported PDFs/spreadsheets

REST/JSON as the primary public and internal API style (Vol. 9 to formalize), with webhooks for outbound event notification to third parties (Vol. 1 BC-19). GraphQL is deferred - not adopted at Release 1.

REST is what most accounting-software integrators (Zoho, QuickBooks, Xero ecosystems) expect; a GraphQL BFF can be layered in later for the web app specifically without changing the underlying service contracts, if UI query flexibility becomes a bottleneck.
Monorepo (single repository) for Release 1, using a build-orchestration tool (e.g. Nx or Turborepo) to manage the module boundaries as enforceable project graph constraints

The modular monolith decision (Vol. 2 Section 2.1) means all 16 bounded contexts deploy together at Release 1 - a monorepo keeps cross-module refactors atomic and lets the shared-types package (Section 2.3) be consumed without a package-publish step. Nx/Turborepo's project-graph lint rules directly enforce Vol. 2 Section 7's "no upward dependency" layering.

When a module is extracted to its own deployable (Vol. 2 Section 2.1 extraction triggers), it can be moved to its own repository without restructuring its internal code, since its folder is already a self-contained Nx/Turborepo project.
/payments-service # Extracted service (CTX-06) - separate deploy from Release 1
/integration-gateway # Extracted service (CTX-16) - separate deploy from Release 1
Per-module internal layering (maps to Vol.2 Sec.6 component diagrams)
/api # Controllers / route handlers - Invoice, Quote, SalesOrder endpoints
/domain # Domain services, aggregates, business rules (invoice lifecycle, Vol.2 Sec.8.1 state machine)
/events # Inbound event handlers (subscribes to InventoryStockChanged, etc.)
/tests # Unit + integration tests for this module only
index.ts # Public export surface - the ONLY thing other modules may import

The index.ts export-surface rule is the enforcement mechanism for Vol. 2's "no cross-module direct database access": a lint rule forbids any import path that reaches past a module's index.ts into its internal folders (e.g. forbidding import ... from '@ledger-platform/receivables/infrastructure/repository' from outside the receivables module).

Standards below are enforced automatically wherever possible (linter rules, CI gate) rather than relying on code review discipline alone. Vol. 8 (Testing & QA) defines the CI pipeline gates that run these checks on every pull request.
A module may only import another module's index.ts export surface, never its internals
ESLint import-boundary rule + Nx module-boundary lint
A module may only depend on modules in a strictly lower architecture layer (Vol. 2 Section 7)
All financial amounts use a Decimal/fixed-point type, never native floating point
Code review + typed wrapper class (Money) required by domain type definitions
All database writes to financial entities go through the domain service layer, never direct repository calls from a controller
Architecture test (dependency-cruiser or similar) run in CI
Every public API endpoint has an OpenAPI schema and a corresponding integration test
CI gate blocks merge if OpenAPI spec or test is missing
Every domain event has a versioned schema in /libs/shared/event-contracts; breaking changes require a new version, not a mutation
No module-level global mutable state; all cross-request state flows through request context (includes tenant ID, user ID, correlation ID - Section 6)
Code review checklist + lint rule against module-scope `let`/mutable exports
Naming: REST resources are plural nouns (/invoices), domain events are past-tense (InvoiceSent), commands are imperative (SendInvoice)
"message": "Invoice INV-0042 is already fully paid and cannot be voided.",

Error codes are namespaced by bounded context (RECEIVABLES., PAYABLES., LEDGER., ...) so client applications and support tooling can route/handle errors predictably. Every error response carries the same correlationId as the structured log entry for that request (Section 6), so a support engineer can go from a user-reported error code straight to the relevant log lines.
Full endpoint-by-endpoint contracts belong to Volume 9; this section fixes the conventions every endpoint must follow so Volume 9 does not have to re-derive them per bounded context.
URL-path versioned: /v1/invoices. A new major version is only introduced for breaking changes; additive fields never bump the version.
Cursor-based (?cursor=...&limit=50), not offset-based, to stay stable under concurrent writes to large tables (e.g. JournalLine).
Consistent query-param syntax: ?filter[status]=Sent&sort=-createdAt across all bounded-context APIs.
All POST endpoints that create a financial transaction accept an Idempotency-Key header, checked against a Redis-backed dedup store (Section 7) before processing.
Bearer JWT (short-lived) issued by CTX-01 Identity & Tenant, containing tenant ID and role claims, validated by every module's API layer via shared middleware.
Every request is scoped to a tenant from the JWT claim, never from a client-supplied tenant ID parameter, to prevent tenant-spoofing (Vol. 6 will formalize this as a security control).
Structured JSON logging only - no unstructured string logs - so logs are queryable in the observability platform (Vol. 7 will select the concrete tool, e.g. an ELK/OpenSearch or vendor SaaS stack).
Every log line carries: tenantId, userId (if applicable), correlationId, module (CTX-xx), and a severity level.

Correlation ID is generated at the API Gateway (Vol. 2 Section 5) on every inbound request and propagated through every internal call and published event, so one user action can be traced end-to-end across modules even inside the monolith.

Financial-mutation log lines (ledger postings, invoice status changes) are never sampled or dropped - they log at INFO level unconditionally, distinct from high-volume debug logs which may be sampled under load.
Unhandled exceptions, failed financial postings, failed external integration calls that could not be retried successfully.
Retried operations, degraded external dependency (e.g. bank feed provider slow), approaching rate limits.
Business-significant events: invoice sent, payment recorded, journal posted, user login. Always includes the correlationId and tenantId.
Detailed request/response payloads, cache hit/miss - disabled in production by default, enabled per-tenant or per-request for support investigations.
Frequently-read, rarely-changed reference data: chart of accounts, tax rate tables, item master, customer list
Write-through: module publishes cache-invalidation on its own domain event (e.g. Inventory invalidates item cache on StockAdjusted)
Time-based TTL (e.g. 5 min) plus explicit invalidation on relevant JournalPosted/InvoiceSent events
JWT refresh-token state, active session list, rate-limit counters
Idempotency-Key deduplication (Section 5) for financial-mutating POST requests
TTL of 24h, matching the client retry window
Sequential number generation (invoice numbers, journal entry numbers) to avoid duplicate numbers under concurrent requests
Lock released immediately after number is reserved
Cache key pattern - tenant ID is always the first segment

No cache key is ever constructed without a tenant ID prefix - this is a CI-enforced lint rule (Section 4) to prevent the single highest-risk multi-tenancy bug class: cross-tenant cache leakage. A shared cache utility wraps all Redis access so individual modules cannot bypass the key convention.
Kafka topics are partitioned by tenantId + aggregateId to guarantee per-aggregate ordering (e.g. all events for one invoice arrive in order) while allowing horizontal consumer scaling across tenants.

At-least-once delivery is assumed for all Kafka consumers (exactly-once is not guaranteed end-to-end across this stack). Every consumer must be idempotent - reprocessing the same event must not double-post a journal entry or double-send a notification.
Idempotency is achieved by consumers tracking a processed-event-id ledger (keyed by event ID, checked before applying side effects) rather than relying on the broker alone.
Poison messages (repeatedly failing to process) are routed to a dead-letter topic after a bounded retry count (e.g. 5 attempts with exponential backoff) and surfaced as an operational alert (Vol. 7).
Another module needs to react to something that already happened
Ledger reacting to InvoiceSent to post revenue
Work that happens on a time schedule, not in reaction to a user action
Recurring invoice generation (Vol.1 BC-16), overdue-invoice reminder emails, nightly report pre-computation
On-demand async job (queue, triggered by user action but too slow to run synchronously)
User-triggered work that would block the HTTP request if run synchronously
Bulk CSV import of items/customers, large report export to PDF/XLSX, bank statement OCR/auto-scan
Cron[Cron Scheduler - e.g. BullMQ repeatable jobs] --> Queue[(Redis-backed Job Queue)]
WorkerPool --> Modules[Calls into module domain services]
WorkerPool --> DLQ[(Dead Letter Queue on repeated failure)]
WorkerPool --> Notify[Notify user/tenant on completion or failure]
Must be idempotent per (tenant, recurrence rule, period) to survive worker retries
Scheduled poll (e.g. every 4h) or provider webhook
Webhook preferred where the provider supports it, to reduce latency and poll cost
Writes to OpenSearch index (Section 2.6), not the transactional DB
Long-running; must support partial-failure recovery (some employees processed, one fails)
Posts journal entries automatically per asset depreciation schedule
Implements the entitlement-check mechanism anticipated by Vol. 1 Section 9 (Standard/Professional/Enterprise editions) and Vol. 2 Section 12's forward contract.
Per-tenant feature-flag set resolved at request time from the tenant record (CTX-01), cached in Redis (Section 7)
Environment variables + a typed config-validation layer at boot (fails fast on missing/invalid config)
Database URL, Kafka brokers, external API keys
Flag-management service (e.g. a lightweight in-house table or a vendor tool) for gradual rollout independent of edition
Enabling the AI Assistant (CTX-15) for a beta cohort before general release
Stored per-tenant in the Identity & Tenant module (CTX-01), read by Automation/Customization (CTX-14)
Custom fields, custom report permissions, workflow rules
(a) Database-per-tenant, (b) Schema-per-tenant within a shared PostgreSQL instance, (c) Shared schema with a tenant_id column on every table plus PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS)

Shared schema + tenant_id column + PostgreSQL RLS for Release 1 and Release 2, with an explicit path to isolate large/Enterprise-edition tenants into dedicated schemas or databases as a scale/compliance option in Release 3

Database-per-tenant does not scale operationally to the thousands-of-tenants target (Vol. 1 Section 2.4 goal) without heavy automation investment the team does not yet have; schema-per-tenant hits connection-pool and migration-fanout limits at similar scale. Shared schema with RLS gives strong logical isolation enforced at the database layer (not just application code), keeps a single migration to run per release, and is the pattern most SaaS platforms at this scale converge on. The Release 3 escape hatch (dedicated schema/database per large tenant) preserves the option for enterprise customers with contractual data-isolation requirements without forcing that cost onto every tenant.

Every table must include a non-nullable tenant_id column and an RLS policy from the very first migration (Vol. 4 to formalize); the application's database connection must set the current tenant context (e.g. via a session variable) on every request so RLS policies apply automatically - this is enforced by shared middleware, not per-query discipline.
participant MW as Tenant Context Middleware
Client->>GW: Request with Bearer JWT (tenantId claim)
MW->>MW: Attach tenantId to correlationId-scoped request context
MW->>DB: Application query (no manual WHERE tenant_id=... needed)
DB-->>MW: RLS automatically filters rows to current_tenant_id
Derived from Vol. 1 Section 2.4 business goals; Volume 7 (Infrastructure) will size the deployment to meet these, and Volume 8 (Testing & QA) will define the load-test suite that verifies them.
Core CRUD operations (invoice create, bill create, journal query) under normal load
Custom report generation against the OpenSearch index (Section 2.6)
Time from domain event publish to downstream consumer completion (e.g. InvoiceSent to JournalPosted)
With Release 3 tenant-isolation escape hatch for largest accounts (Section 11)
Recurring invoice batch for 10,000 tenants completes within a 1-hour nightly window
Vol. 2 Section 2.1 modular monolith decision, Section 7 module dependency graph
Vol. 1 Section 2.4 Business Goals & Success Measures
Confirm cloud provider preference (AWS/GCP/Azure) if one exists - this affects which managed services (Kafka, Redis, OpenSearch, object storage) Volume 7 will size and price.
Confirm whether Nx or Turborepo is preferred for the monorepo tooling, or whether the team has an existing preference/expertise to weigh in.

Confirm the Release 3 tenant-isolation escape hatch (Section 11.1) is an acceptable answer to any known enterprise-customer data-residency requirements, or whether isolation needs to be available earlier.

On approval, Phase 4 begins: Data Architecture (Volume 4) - entity-relationship diagrams, physical schema design (including the tenant_id/RLS pattern from Section 11), indexing and partitioning strategy for high-volume tables, and the migration strategy, all built on the module boundaries (Vol. 2) and stack decisions (this volume).
